name: Human Development
description: A lifespan survey of physical, cognitive, and socioemotional development from conception through death, integrating theories of Piaget, Erikson, Vygotsky, and Bronfenbrenner.

modules.title: Theories of Development
modules.titleZh: 发展理论（皮亚杰、埃里克森、维果茨基）
modules.objectives: Compare Piaget's cognitive stages, Erikson's psychosocial stages, and Vygotsky's sociocultural theory; evaluate each theory's contributions and limitations.
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Erikson's Stages of Psychosocial Development
modules.assignment: Create a three-column comparison chart contrasting Piaget, Erikson, and Vygotsky on: unit of analysis, primary driving force, stages or mechanisms, and educational implications. Write a 250-word reflection on which theory you find most convincing and why.

modules.title: Prenatal Development & Birth
modules.objectives: Describe the three stages of prenatal development (germinal, embryonic, fetal), identify teratogens and their critical periods, and explain the birth process and its psychological dimensions.
modules.assignment: Research one teratogen (such as alcohol, cocaine, or Zika virus) and write a 350-word analysis of its mechanism of harm, the critical period of greatest vulnerability, and the developmental outcomes for the child at different prenatal stages of exposure.

modules.title: Infancy: Physical & Cognitive Development
modules.titleZh: 婴儿期：身体与认知发展
modules.objectives: Describe major physical milestones of infancy, explain the development of sensory and motor abilities, and apply Piaget's sensorimotor stage including object permanence.
modules.assignment: Interview a parent of a child under 2 or review an infant development case study. Document five observed behaviors and identify which sub-stage of Piaget's sensorimotor period they reflect. Write a 300-word developmental narrative.

modules.title: Infancy: Social & Emotional Development
modules.titleZh: 婴儿期：社会与情绪发展
modules.objectives: Describe temperament types, explain Bowlby's attachment theory, distinguish secure from insecure attachment patterns, and describe the development of social referencing and basic emotions.
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Attachment & Early Social Development
modules.assignment: Using Ainsworth's Strange Situation categories, classify a scenario you read about or observe (in person, or from a developmental psychology case study). Write a 300-word analysis connecting the attachment pattern to specific caregiver behaviors described.

modules.title: Early Childhood: Cognitive Development
modules.objectives: Describe cognitive characteristics of the preoperational stage including symbolic thinking, egocentrism, and animism; explain theory of mind development and Vygotsky's ZPD in early education.
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Theory of Mind in Children
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Early Childhood Cognitive Practice
modules.assignment: Administer a simple false-belief task to a 3- and a 5-year-old (or review published findings). Write a 350-word analysis of whether each child passed or failed and what this reveals about their theory of mind development.

modules.title: Early Childhood: Social-Emotional Development
modules.objectives: Describe Erikson's initiative vs. guilt stage, explain parenting styles (Baumrind) and their outcomes, and analyze the role of play in social-emotional development.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Psychology 2e Ch 9.2 — Parenting & Social Development
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Social Development in Early Childhood
modules.assignment: Reflect on the parenting style (authoritative, authoritarian, permissive, or uninvolved) used in your own upbringing. Write a 350-word analysis connecting that style to Baumrind's research outcomes and your own social-emotional development.

modules.title: Language Development
modules.objectives: Trace the milestones of language development from cooing to complex syntax, evaluate nativist (Chomsky) vs. social-interactionist (Vygotsky) explanations, and explain bilingualism and critical periods.
modules.assignment: Compare two theories of language acquisition (nativist and social-interactionist) in a 350-word essay. Present evidence for each and argue which theory better explains bilingual children's language acquisition.

modules.title: Middle Childhood: School & Learning
modules.objectives: Describe Piaget's concrete operational stage abilities (conservation, classification, seriation), apply Erikson's industry vs. inferiority stage, and explain the role of school, peers, and achievement motivation.
modules.assignment: Interview an elementary-school-aged child about a conservation task (quantity of liquid or clay). Write a 300-word report of the child's reasoning process and connect it to Piaget's concrete operational stage characteristics.

modules.title: Adolescence: Physical & Cognitive Development
modules.titleZh: 青少年期：身体与认知发展
modules.objectives: Describe the physical changes of puberty and the adolescent brain, explain Piaget's formal operational stage including hypothetical-deductive reasoning, and discuss adolescent risk-taking.
modules.assignment: Design a 300-word educational program for parents explaining adolescent brain development, specifically why teens engage in risk-taking. Include at least three evidence-based recommendations for parenting strategies that work with — not against — adolescent brain development.

modules.title: Adolescence: Identity & Social Development
modules.titleZh: 青少年期：身份认同与社会发展
modules.objectives: Apply Erikson's identity vs. role confusion stage, describe Marcia's four identity statuses, and analyze peer influence, romantic relationships, and moral development in adolescence.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Psychology 2e Ch 9.3 — Adolescent Social Development
modules.assignment: Classify yourself or a fictional teenage character using Marcia's four identity statuses (achievement, foreclosure, moratorium, diffusion) for three domains: career, religion, and politics. Write a 350-word justification for each classification.

modules.title: Emerging Adulthood
modules.objectives: Define emerging adulthood as a distinct developmental stage, describe Arnett's five characteristics, and analyze identity exploration, educational transitions, and the delay of traditional adult milestones.
modules.assignment: Assess which of Arnett's five characteristics of emerging adulthood (identity exploration, instability, self-focus, feeling in-between, possibilities) apply most to your current life. Write a 350-word personal narrative connecting your experiences to the theory.

modules.title: Adult Development
modules.objectives: Apply Erikson's stages of intimacy vs. isolation and generativity vs. stagnation, describe physical and cognitive changes in early and middle adulthood, and evaluate the role of work and family in adult identity.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Psychology 2e Ch 9.4 — Middle & Early Adulthood
modules.assignment: Interview an adult between 30–55 years of age about their sense of purpose in work, family, and community. Write a 400-word analysis applying Erikson's generativity vs. stagnation stage and Levinson's seasons of life to their responses.

modules.title: Late Adulthood & Aging
modules.objectives: Describe physical, sensory, and cognitive changes in late adulthood; apply Erikson's integrity vs. despair stage; and evaluate theories of successful aging including activity and socioemotional selectivity theory.
modules.assignment: Interview an older adult (60+) about how their priorities and relationships have changed over time. Write a 400-word analysis connecting their responses to socioemotional selectivity theory and Erikson's integrity vs. despair.

modules.title: Death, Dying & Grief
modules.objectives: Describe Kübler-Ross's five stages of grief, explain contemporary models of bereavement (dual process model), discuss cultural variations in death practices, and apply palliative care psychology.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Psychology 2e Ch 9.5 — End of Life
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Late Adulthood & Death Practice
modules.assignment: Apply Kübler-Ross's stages to a personal or widely reported experience of loss (the death of a public figure, fictional character, or your own loss). Write a 400-word reflective essay evaluating whether the stages model accurately captures the grief process, using at least one critique from the contemporary grief literature.

quizQuestions.question: Piaget's theory of cognitive development is best described as:
options: A stage theory based on qualitatively different ways of thinking
A behavioral theory based on reinforcement
A nativist theory of language acquisition
quizQuestions.answer: A stage theory based on qualitatively different ways of thinking
quizQuestions.explanation: Piaget proposed that children progress through four universal stages, each representing qualitatively different cognitive structures.

quizQuestions.question: Vygotsky's zone of proximal development emphasizes the role of:
options: Social interaction and scaffolding in learning
quizQuestions.answer: Social interaction and scaffolding in learning
quizQuestions.explanation: Vygotsky's ZPD is the gap between what a child can do alone and what they can achieve with social guidance (scaffolding).

quizQuestions.question: Erikson's psychosocial theory differs from Freud's psychosexual theory in that it:
options: Spans the entire lifespan with social challenges at each stage
quizQuestions.answer: Spans the entire lifespan with social challenges at each stage
quizQuestions.explanation: Erikson extended Freud's model across the entire lifespan, with each stage centered on a psychosocial conflict shaped by social relationships.

quizQuestions.question: Bronfenbrenner's ecological systems theory proposes that development is influenced by:
options: Multiple nested systems from microsystem to macrosystem
A fixed sequence of biological stages
quizQuestions.answer: Multiple nested systems from microsystem to macrosystem
quizQuestions.explanation: Bronfenbrenner's model identifies concentric layers of environmental influence from immediate family (microsystem) to cultural context (macrosystem).

quizQuestions.question: The nature vs. nurture debate in developmental psychology concerns:
options: The role of schools vs. parents
Whether development is driven by genetic inheritance or environmental experience
quizQuestions.answer: Whether development is driven by genetic inheritance or environmental experience
quizQuestions.explanation: The nature-nurture debate asks how much of development is determined by genes (nature) vs. experiences and environment (nurture).

quizQuestions.question: Teratogens are substances or agents that:
options: Cause birth defects or developmental abnormalities
quizQuestions.answer: Cause birth defects or developmental abnormalities
quizQuestions.explanation: Teratogens (drugs, diseases, radiation) interfere with normal prenatal development and can cause structural or functional birth defects.

quizQuestions.question: The germinal stage of prenatal development lasts approximately:
options: The first two weeks after fertilization
quizQuestions.answer: The first two weeks after fertilization
quizQuestions.explanation: The germinal stage covers fertilization through implantation of the blastocyst — approximately the first two weeks.

quizQuestions.question: Fetal Alcohol Spectrum Disorder (FASD) is caused by:
quizQuestions.answer: Prenatal alcohol exposure
quizQuestions.explanation: FASD is caused by alcohol crossing the placenta and disrupting fetal brain development, causing lifelong cognitive and behavioral impairments.

quizQuestions.question: Critical periods in prenatal development refer to times when:
options: Specific organs are most vulnerable to teratogen damage
The sex of the fetus is determined
quizQuestions.answer: Specific organs are most vulnerable to teratogen damage
quizQuestions.explanation: Critical periods are windows of peak vulnerability when specific organ systems are forming and most susceptible to teratogenic disruption.

quizQuestions.question: The embryonic stage (weeks 3–8) is particularly critical because:
options: The fetus gains the most weight
All major organ systems are forming during this period
quizQuestions.answer: All major organ systems are forming during this period
quizQuestions.explanation: Organogenesis (formation of all major organs and systems) occurs in weeks 3–8, making this the period of greatest teratogenic vulnerability.

quizQuestions.question: Object permanence typically develops during which sub-stage of Piaget's sensorimotor period?
quizQuestions.answer: 8–12 months
quizQuestions.explanation: Object permanence — knowing objects exist when hidden — typically emerges around 8 months in Piaget's sensorimotor stage.

quizQuestions.question: The rooting reflex in newborns is best described as:
options: Turning toward a touch on the cheek and beginning to suck
Curling fingers around objects placed in the palm
quizQuestions.answer: Turning toward a cheek touch and beginning to suck
quizQuestions.explanation: The rooting reflex causes infants to turn toward a cheek touch — an adaptive reflex that facilitates breastfeeding.

quizQuestions.question: Habituation in infants is used by researchers to assess:
options: Cognitive abilities such as memory and discrimination
quizQuestions.answer: Cognitive abilities such as memory and discrimination
quizQuestions.explanation: Researchers use habituation (decreased response to repeated stimuli) and dishabituation (renewed response to novelty) to infer infant cognition.

quizQuestions.question: Gross motor development in infancy generally proceeds in which direction?
options: Distal to proximal and feet to head
quizQuestions.answer: Cephalocaudal and proximodistal
quizQuestions.explanation: Motor development proceeds cephalocaudal (head to toe) and proximodistal (center outward), reflecting neural maturation patterns.

quizQuestions.question: Deferred imitation in infancy (imitating an action seen hours or days earlier) demonstrates:
options: The beginnings of symbolic representation and memory
quizQuestions.answer: The beginnings of symbolic representation and memory
quizQuestions.explanation: Deferred imitation requires a mental representation of the observed action — evidence of early symbolic thinking and memory.

quizQuestions.question: Ainsworth's Strange Situation procedure was designed to measure:
quizQuestions.answer: Quality of infant-caregiver attachment
quizQuestions.explanation: The Strange Situation is a structured laboratory procedure assessing attachment security by observing infant reactions to separation and reunion.

quizQuestions.question: Securely attached infants, when reunited with their caregiver after separation, typically:
options: Show no reaction to the caregiver's return
Seek contact, are comforted, and return to play
quizQuestions.answer: Seek contact, are comforted, and return to play
quizQuestions.explanation: Secure infants use the caregiver as a safe haven — they seek comfort upon reunion, are soothed, and can resume exploration.

quizQuestions.question: Thomas and Chess's 'easy' temperament type is characterized by:
options: Irregular routines, slow adaptation, and intense negative mood
Regular routines, positive mood, and easy adaptation to new situations
Slow adaptation but mild emotional reactions
Intense reactions to novelty and unpredictable schedules
quizQuestions.answer: Regular routines, positive mood, and easy adaptation to new situations
quizQuestions.explanation: Easy temperament infants have predictable routines, positive affect, and adapt readily to new experiences — approximately 40% of infants.

quizQuestions.question: Social referencing in infancy refers to:
options: Looking to a caregiver's emotional signals to guide behavior in ambiguous situations
Following a caregiver's gaze toward an object
Recognizing one's reflection in a mirror
quizQuestions.answer: Looking to a caregiver's emotional signals to guide behavior in ambiguous situations
quizQuestions.explanation: Social referencing is the infant's use of caregiver facial expressions and voice to decide how to respond to unfamiliar or uncertain situations.

quizQuestions.question: The internal working model developed through early attachment experiences:
options: Shapes expectations about relationships and self-worth throughout life
Only affects romantic relationships in adulthood
Has no connection to cognitive development
quizQuestions.answer: Shapes expectations about relationships and self-worth throughout life
quizQuestions.explanation: Bowlby proposed that early attachment creates internal working models — mental representations of relationships that guide future expectations.

quizQuestions.question: Animism, typical of the preoperational stage, refers to children's belief that:
options: Inanimate objects have feelings and intentions
quizQuestions.answer: Inanimate objects have feelings and intentions
quizQuestions.explanation: Preoperational animism is the attribution of life and mental states to inanimate objects (e.g., 'the rock is angry').

quizQuestions.question: Theory of mind (ToM) refers to the ability to:
options: Understand that others have mental states different from one's own
quizQuestions.answer: Understand that others have mental states different from one's own
quizQuestions.explanation: ToM is the cognitive ability to attribute mental states (beliefs, desires, intentions) to others and understand they differ from one's own.

quizQuestions.question: Scaffolding in education, based on Vygotsky's theory, involves:
options: Giving children complete answers to problems
Providing temporary support that is gradually withdrawn as competence develops
Teaching only in the native language
quizQuestions.answer: Providing temporary support that is gradually withdrawn as competence develops
quizQuestions.explanation: Scaffolding is the adult support (hints, modeling, questions) within the ZPD that is gradually removed as the child becomes more competent.

quizQuestions.question: Centration, a characteristic of preoperational thinking, refers to:
options: Focusing on multiple dimensions of a problem simultaneously
Focusing on only one aspect of a situation while ignoring others
Understanding that actions can be mentally reversed
quizQuestions.answer: Focusing on only one aspect of a situation while ignoring others
quizQuestions.explanation: Centration causes preoperational children to focus on one perceptual feature (e.g., height of liquid) while ignoring others (e.g., width).

quizQuestions.question: Symbolic play (pretend play) begins in which developmental stage?
quizQuestions.answer: Preoperational
quizQuestions.explanation: Symbolic/pretend play emerges during the preoperational stage as children develop the ability to use one thing to represent another.

quizQuestions.question: Baumrind's authoritative parenting style is characterized by:
options: High demands, low warmth, and strict obedience
High demands combined with high warmth, explanations, and responsiveness
quizQuestions.answer: High demands combined with high warmth, explanations, and responsiveness
quizQuestions.explanation: Authoritative parents set clear standards while being warm, responsive, and willing to explain rules — associated with best outcomes.

quizQuestions.question: Erikson's initiative vs. guilt stage occurs during:
quizQuestions.answer: Early childhood (approximately ages 3–6)
quizQuestions.explanation: Erikson's third stage occurs in the preschool years when children's expanding abilities create initiative, which guilt can suppress if overdisciplined.

quizQuestions.question: Cooperative play, in which children play with shared goals and rules, typically emerges:
options: Around ages 4–6 in the preschool years
quizQuestions.answer: Around ages 4–6 in the preschool years
quizQuestions.explanation: Cooperative play with shared goals and rule-governed interaction develops during the preschool years as social cognition matures.

quizQuestions.question: Authoritarian parenting (high demands, low warmth) is most associated with children who:
options: Are obedient but may have lower self-esteem and social competence
quizQuestions.answer: Are obedient but may have lower self-esteem and social competence
quizQuestions.explanation: Authoritarian parenting produces compliant children but is associated with lower self-esteem and weaker social skills compared to authoritative parenting.

quizQuestions.question: Parallel play, where children play near but not with each other, is most typical of:
quizQuestions.answer: Toddlers and young preschoolers
quizQuestions.explanation: Parallel play, where children engage in similar activities side-by-side without true cooperation, is characteristic of toddlers approximately 18 months–3 years.

quizQuestions.question: Infants typically produce their first words around:
quizQuestions.answer: 10–13 months
quizQuestions.explanation: First words — typically nouns referring to important people and objects — emerge around 10–13 months of age.

quizQuestions.question: The language acquisition device (LAD) proposed by Chomsky is a theoretical:
options: Innate brain structure predisposing humans to acquire language
quizQuestions.answer: Innate brain structure predisposing humans to acquire language
quizQuestions.explanation: Chomsky's LAD is a hypothetical innate mechanism that contains universal grammar, enabling rapid language acquisition in children.

quizQuestions.question: The telegraphic speech stage (e.g., 'more milk,' 'daddy go') typically occurs around:
quizQuestions.answer: 18–24 months
quizQuestions.explanation: Telegraphic speech — two-word utterances containing content words without grammatical markers — emerges around 18–24 months.

quizQuestions.question: Overregularization errors (e.g., 'I goed to school') in child language demonstrate:
options: Active rule learning and application of grammatical patterns
quizQuestions.answer: Active rule learning and application of grammatical patterns
quizQuestions.explanation: Overregularization shows children are actively extracting and applying grammar rules, not just imitating — a sign of normal language development.

quizQuestions.question: The critical period hypothesis for language acquisition proposes that full language mastery:
options: Is equally possible at any age
Is most easily achieved during a sensitive period in childhood before puberty
quizQuestions.answer: Is most easily achieved during a sensitive period in childhood before puberty
quizQuestions.explanation: Lenneberg proposed a critical/sensitive period for language acquisition ending around puberty, after which full native-like mastery becomes difficult.

quizQuestions.question: Conservation, mastered in the concrete operational stage, is the understanding that:
options: Objects can be classified into categories
Two objects can be in the same place
Quantity remains constant despite changes in shape or arrangement
Actions can be performed in sequence
quizQuestions.answer: Quantity remains constant despite changes in shape or arrangement
quizQuestions.explanation: Conservation (Piaget) is the recognition that amount, number, or volume does not change when its appearance is altered.

quizQuestions.question: Erikson's industry vs. inferiority stage corresponds to:
quizQuestions.answer: Middle childhood (ages 6–12)
quizQuestions.explanation: Erikson's fourth stage occurs during the school years; children develop competence (industry) through mastery of skills or feel inferior when they fail.

quizQuestions.question: Seriation in the concrete operational stage refers to the ability to:
options: Arrange objects in order along a dimension such as height or weight
quizQuestions.answer: Arrange objects in order along a dimension such as height or weight
quizQuestions.explanation: Seriation is the ability to mentally arrange a set of objects in order along a quantitative dimension — mastered in concrete operations.

quizQuestions.question: Peer relationships in middle childhood become especially important for developing:
quizQuestions.answer: Social comparison and self-concept
quizQuestions.explanation: During middle childhood, children increasingly evaluate themselves through social comparison with same-age peers, shaping self-concept.

quizQuestions.question: Kohlberg's preconventional level of moral reasoning (typical of children) is based on:
quizQuestions.answer: Avoiding punishment and seeking rewards
quizQuestions.explanation: Preconventional morality judges right and wrong by consequences to oneself — avoiding punishment (Stage 1) and gaining rewards (Stage 2).

quizQuestions.question: The first sign of puberty in girls is typically:
quizQuestions.answer: Breast development (thelarche)
quizQuestions.explanation: In most girls, breast development (thelarche) is the first observable sign of puberty, typically beginning around age 8–13.

quizQuestions.question: Hypothetico-deductive reasoning, characteristic of formal operations, involves:
options: Solving concrete problems with objects present
Systematically testing all possible solutions to a problem
quizQuestions.answer: Systematically testing all possible solutions to a problem
quizQuestions.explanation: Formal operational thinkers can generate hypotheses and systematically test all possible variables — abstract, scientific reasoning.

quizQuestions.question: The personal fable phenomenon in adolescence is the belief that:
options: One's experiences and feelings are uniquely special and not understood by others
Peers are more important than family
quizQuestions.answer: One's experiences and feelings are uniquely special and not understood by others
quizQuestions.explanation: The personal fable is adolescent egocentrism — the conviction that one's experiences are unique, contributing to risk-taking ('it won't happen to me').

quizQuestions.question: Early maturation in boys is generally associated with:
options: Higher status, self-confidence, and athletic success in the short term
quizQuestions.answer: Higher status, self-confidence, and athletic success in the short term
quizQuestions.explanation: Early-maturing boys often gain social advantages in the short term through physical advantage, though some show later adjustment difficulties.

quizQuestions.question: The prefrontal cortex in adolescence is still developing, which contributes to:
options: Poor impulse control and greater sensitivity to peer influence
quizQuestions.answer: Poor impulse control and greater sensitivity to peer influence
quizQuestions.explanation: The immature prefrontal cortex limits inhibitory control while the reward-seeking limbic system is fully active, explaining adolescent risk-taking.

quizQuestions.question: Identity moratorium (Marcia) describes an adolescent who is:
options: Committed to an identity without exploration
Actively exploring identity options without yet committing
quizQuestions.answer: Actively exploring identity options without yet committing
quizQuestions.explanation: Moratorium is the active exploration of identity alternatives without having made a commitment — often the healthiest stage for adolescents.

quizQuestions.question: Identity foreclosure occurs when an adolescent:
options: Is committed to an identity chosen after thorough exploration
Adopts an identity without personal exploration, typically given by parents
Is in the process of active identity exploration
quizQuestions.answer: Adopts an identity without personal exploration, typically given by parents
quizQuestions.explanation: Foreclosed adolescents commit to an identity (often parental) without exploring alternatives — showing premature closure.

quizQuestions.question: Kohlberg's conventional level of morality (common in adolescence and adulthood) bases moral judgments on:
options: Social rules, laws, and maintaining relationships
quizQuestions.answer: Social rules, laws, and maintaining relationships
quizQuestions.explanation: Conventional morality orients to social norms — 'good person' behavior (Stage 3) and law and order (Stage 4).

quizQuestions.question: Clique membership in adolescence typically provides:
options: A small intimate peer group offering belonging and shared identity
quizQuestions.answer: A small intimate peer group offering belonging and shared identity
quizQuestions.explanation: Cliques are small (6–10 person) same-sex or mixed peer groups that provide belonging, social comparison, and identity anchoring in early adolescence.

quizQuestions.question: Peer conformity pressure in adolescence peaks around:
quizQuestions.explanation: Research suggests susceptibility to peer conformity pressure peaks around 14–15 years, then gradually declines as identity becomes more consolidated.

quizQuestions.question: Jeffrey Arnett defined emerging adulthood as the period from approximately:
quizQuestions.answer: 18–25 years
quizQuestions.explanation: Arnett described emerging adulthood as a distinct developmental period from approximately 18 to 25 years characterized by identity exploration.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is NOT one of Arnett's five characteristics of emerging adulthood?
options: Settled stability in career and family
quizQuestions.answer: Settled stability in career and family
quizQuestions.explanation: Arnett's five features are identity exploration, instability, self-focus, feeling in-between, and possibilities — settled stability is characteristic of established adulthood.

quizQuestions.question: Erikson's stage for early adulthood centers on:
quizQuestions.answer: Intimacy vs. isolation
quizQuestions.explanation: Erikson's sixth stage (early adulthood) involves the challenge of forming close, committed relationships vs. remaining emotionally isolated.

quizQuestions.question: The delay of marriage and parenthood in emerging adulthood compared to previous generations is most associated with:
options: Extended education, economic factors, and identity exploration
quizQuestions.answer: Extended education, economic factors, and identity exploration
quizQuestions.explanation: Rising education requirements, economic instability, and the prioritization of identity exploration have pushed traditional adult milestones later.

quizQuestions.question: College attendance is considered developmentally significant in emerging adulthood primarily because it:
options: Provides a structured moratorium for identity exploration
quizQuestions.answer: Provides a structured moratorium for identity exploration
quizQuestions.explanation: College provides a socially sanctioned context to explore values, relationships, and possible selves while maintaining institutional support.

quizQuestions.question: Generativity, in Erikson's theory, refers to:
options: A concern for guiding, nurturing, and contributing to the next generation
quizQuestions.answer: A concern for guiding, nurturing, and contributing to the next generation
quizQuestions.explanation: Generativity (Erikson's 7th stage) involves investing in mentoring, parenting, and contributing to society for future generations.

quizQuestions.question: The midlife crisis concept, popularized in Western culture, is:
options: Supported as a universal stage by developmental psychology research
Not well supported as a universal stage; most adults report midlife satisfaction
quizQuestions.answer: Not well supported as a universal stage; most adults report midlife satisfaction
quizQuestions.explanation: Research shows that a true midlife crisis is the exception, not the rule — most middle-aged adults report stability and satisfaction.

quizQuestions.question: Crystallized intelligence in adulthood:
options: Remains stable or increases through most of adulthood
quizQuestions.answer: Remains stable or increases through most of adulthood
quizQuestions.explanation: Crystallized intelligence — accumulated knowledge and verbal skills — tends to remain stable or increase through middle and late adulthood.

quizQuestions.question: The empty nest transition in middle adulthood is often associated with:
options: Increased marital satisfaction for many couples
quizQuestions.answer: Increased marital satisfaction for many couples
quizQuestions.explanation: Research finds that many couples experience renewed satisfaction in their relationship after children leave home, though adjustment varies.

quizQuestions.question: Processing speed as a cognitive ability in adulthood:
options: Peaks in early adulthood and declines gradually across the lifespan
quizQuestions.answer: Peaks in early adulthood and declines gradually across the lifespan
quizQuestions.explanation: Processing speed peaks in the early 20s and shows gradual but consistent decline across adulthood — one of the earliest cognitive changes.

quizQuestions.question: Erikson's final psychosocial stage, integrity vs. despair, involves:
options: Reviewing life with acceptance or regret
Finding purpose in work and family
quizQuestions.answer: Reviewing life with acceptance or regret
quizQuestions.explanation: Integrity vs. despair involves reflecting on one's life — achieving acceptance (integrity) or feeling regret and hopelessness (despair).

quizQuestions.question: Socioemotional selectivity theory (Carstensen) predicts that older adults prioritize:
options: Meeting as many new people as possible
Emotionally meaningful close relationships over new acquaintances
quizQuestions.answer: Emotionally meaningful close relationships over new acquaintances
quizQuestions.explanation: SST holds that as time perspective shortens with age, people prioritize emotionally rewarding close relationships over information-seeking or novelty.

quizQuestions.question: Dementia differs from normal aging cognitive changes in that it:
options: Is present in all people over 80
Involves significant memory loss that interferes with daily functioning
quizQuestions.answer: Involves significant memory loss that interferes with daily functioning
quizQuestions.explanation: Dementia (e.g., Alzheimer's) causes progressive cognitive decline that impairs daily functioning — distinct from normal age-related memory changes.

quizQuestions.question: Activity theory of aging proposes that older adults who remain:
options: Socially and physically active maintain higher life satisfaction
Focused on reflection rather than activity age most successfully
Physically but not socially active maintain best health
quizQuestions.answer: Socially and physically active maintain higher life satisfaction
quizQuestions.explanation: Activity theory holds that maintaining social roles and activities is associated with greater well-being and life satisfaction in late adulthood.

quizQuestions.question: The oldest-old (age 85+) are the fastest-growing segment of the population primarily due to:
options: Increased birth rates in that cohort
Advances in medical care, nutrition, and sanitation
quizQuestions.answer: Advances in medical care, nutrition, and sanitation
quizQuestions.explanation: The growth of the oldest-old is driven by increased life expectancy from improvements in healthcare, nutrition, and public health.

quizQuestions.question: Kübler-Ross's stage model of grief includes all of the following EXCEPT:
quizQuestions.answer: Guilt
quizQuestions.explanation: Kübler-Ross's five stages are Denial, Anger, Bargaining, Depression, and Acceptance. Guilt is not one of the five original stages.

quizQuestions.question: A key criticism of Kübler-Ross's stage model is that:
options: It focuses too much on culture
Grief stages are not universal and people do not necessarily progress through them in order
It ignores the role of religion
It only applies to deaths by illness
quizQuestions.answer: Grief stages are not universal and people do not necessarily progress through them in order
quizQuestions.explanation: Research shows grief is highly variable and non-linear; many people do not experience all five stages or experience them in a different order.

quizQuestions.question: The dual process model of bereavement (Stroebe & Schut) proposes that grievers oscillate between:
quizQuestions.answer: Loss-oriented and restoration-oriented coping
quizQuestions.explanation: The dual process model holds that healthy grieving involves oscillating between confronting grief (loss-oriented) and attending to life changes (restoration-oriented).

quizQuestions.question: Palliative care focuses primarily on:
options: Relieving pain and improving quality of life for those with serious illness
quizQuestions.answer: Relieving pain and improving quality of life for those with serious illness
quizQuestions.explanation: Palliative care provides symptom relief, emotional support, and quality-of-life improvement for patients with serious illness, regardless of prognosis.

quizQuestions.question: Terror management theory proposes that awareness of death motivates people to:
options: Invest in cultural worldviews and self-esteem to buffer death anxiety
quizQuestions.answer: Invest in cultural worldviews and self-esteem to buffer death anxiety
quizQuestions.explanation: TMT (Greenberg, Pyszczynski, Solomon) holds that cultural worldviews and personal significance provide symbolic immortality, reducing death terror.

questions.question: Piaget's concept of assimilation refers to:
options: Changing a schema to fit new information
Incorporating new information into an existing schema
Moving to a new cognitive stage
questions.answer: Incorporating new information into an existing schema
questions.explanation: Assimilation is the process of interpreting new experiences through an existing schema without changing the schema.

questions.question: Which teratogen causes the most widespread preventable developmental disability in Western countries?
questions.answer: Alcohol
questions.explanation: Fetal Alcohol Spectrum Disorder (FASD) is the leading preventable cause of intellectual disability and developmental problems in the Western world.

questions.question: Object permanence develops during Piaget's:
questions.answer: Sensorimotor stage
questions.explanation: Object permanence — knowing objects continue to exist when out of sight — develops during the sensorimotor stage, around 8 months.

questions.question: Disorganized attachment in infancy is most associated with:
options: Frightening or frightened caregiver behavior and abuse
questions.answer: Frightening or frightened caregiver behavior and abuse
questions.explanation: Disorganized attachment occurs when the caregiver is both the source of safety and fear — common in maltreating or traumatized caregiver relationships.

questions.question: Theory of mind is typically well-established by age:
questions.answer: 4–5 years
questions.explanation: Most children pass standard false-belief tasks by age 4–5, indicating a functional theory of mind has developed.

questions.question: Authoritative parenting is associated with which child outcomes?
options: High self-esteem, academic achievement, and social competence
questions.answer: High self-esteem, academic achievement, and social competence
questions.explanation: Baumrind's research and subsequent studies consistently link authoritative parenting to the most positive developmental outcomes.

questions.question: Overextension in early language development refers to:
options: Using a word too narrowly for only one object
Using a word to refer to a broader category than its correct meaning
questions.answer: Using a word to refer to a broader category than its correct meaning
questions.explanation: Overextension is applying a word too broadly — calling all men 'daddy' or all round objects 'ball.'

questions.question: Conservation of liquid quantity is mastered during Piaget's:
questions.answer: Concrete operational stage
questions.explanation: Conservation of liquid is one of the early achievements of the concrete operational stage, typically mastered around age 7–8.

questions.question: Hypothetico-deductive reasoning first emerges during:
options: Adolescence in the formal operational stage
questions.answer: Adolescence in the formal operational stage
questions.explanation: The ability to generate and systematically test hypotheses is the hallmark of formal operations, emerging in adolescence.

questions.question: Identity diffusion (Marcia) is characterized by:
options: Adoption of others' identity without exploration
questions.answer: Neither exploration nor commitment
questions.explanation: Diffusion represents the absence of both exploration and commitment — the person has not engaged with identity questions.

questions.question: Arnett's emerging adulthood theory has been criticized primarily because:
options: It covers too short a period
It may apply mainly to middle-class Western youth with access to higher education
It underestimates the role of parents
questions.answer: It may apply mainly to middle-class Western youth with access to higher education
questions.explanation: Critics note that the extended moratorium of emerging adulthood is a luxury not available to youth who must work or marry early due to economic necessity.

questions.question: Generativity vs. stagnation (Erikson's 7th stage) centers on the question:
options: Will I produce something of lasting value for future generations?
questions.answer: Will I produce something of lasting value for future generations?
questions.explanation: Generativity involves caring for and contributing to the next generation through parenting, mentoring, creativity, or community involvement.

questions.question: Socioemotional selectivity theory predicts that as people age:
options: They seek increasingly large social networks
They prioritize emotionally meaningful relationships and present-focused goals
questions.answer: They prioritize emotionally meaningful relationships and present-focused goals
questions.explanation: When time feels limited, SST predicts a shift toward emotional and social goals over exploratory or knowledge-acquisition goals.

questions.question: The dual process model of grief suggests that healthy bereavement involves:
options: Remaining focused on the loss at all times
Alternating between grief processing and engaging with life restoration tasks
Quickly moving past emotions to practical tasks
questions.answer: Alternating between grief processing and engaging with life restoration tasks
questions.explanation: The dual process model proposes that oscillating between loss-oriented (grief) and restoration-oriented (life rebuilding) coping is adaptive.

questions.question: Vygotsky's emphasis on the ZPD has the most direct implications for:
options: Designing educational environments with adult scaffolding
Understanding the role of reinforcement in learning
questions.answer: Designing educational environments with adult scaffolding
questions.explanation: The ZPD implies that optimal instruction targets skills slightly above current ability, with expert guidance (scaffolding) to support acquisition.

questions.question: Accommodation in Piaget's theory involves:
options: Fitting new information into existing schemas
Modifying existing schemas to fit new information
Reaching a state of cognitive balance
questions.answer: Modifying existing schemas to fit new information
questions.explanation: Accommodation is changing or creating a new schema when existing ones cannot assimilate new information.

questions.question: The embryonic stage is most vulnerable to teratogens because:
options: The placenta is not yet formed
The immune system is weakest then
All major organ systems are undergoing rapid formation (organogenesis)
The fetus is too small to be affected by larger doses
questions.answer: All major organ systems are undergoing rapid formation (organogenesis)
questions.explanation: Organogenesis occurs in weeks 3–8 — the period of greatest teratogenic risk for organ malformation.

questions.question: The Moro (startle) reflex in newborns is adaptive because:
options: It may have evolved to help infants cling to caregivers when startled
questions.answer: It may have evolved to help infants cling to caregivers when startled
questions.explanation: The Moro reflex — arms spread then pulled in — is thought to be an evolutionary remnant helping young primates grasp the mother when startled.

questions.question: Secure attachment in infancy is most strongly predicted by:
options: Caregiver sensitivity and responsiveness to infant signals
questions.answer: Caregiver sensitivity and responsiveness to infant signals
questions.explanation: Research consistently identifies caregiver sensitivity — reading and responding promptly and appropriately to infant cues — as the strongest predictor of secure attachment.

questions.question: Children with autism spectrum disorder typically show impaired:
options: Theory of mind and social cognition
questions.answer: Theory of mind and social cognition
questions.explanation: Baron-Cohen's work linked ASD to 'mindblindness' — difficulty understanding others' mental states (theory of mind deficits).

questions.question: Which parenting style is most associated with the highest rates of juvenile delinquency?
questions.answer: Uninvolved (neglectful)
questions.explanation: Uninvolved parenting, characterized by low warmth and low control, is most strongly associated with behavioral problems and delinquency.

questions.question: A child who points to a cat in a book and says 'doggy' is demonstrating:
questions.answer: Overextension
questions.explanation: Overextension applies a word (here, 'doggy') to objects beyond its correct referent — a common early vocabulary error.

questions.question: Kohlberg's postconventional morality involves:
options: Abstract principles of justice that may transcend law
questions.answer: Abstract principles of justice that may transcend law
questions.explanation: Postconventional morality (Stages 5–6) uses abstract ethical principles (justice, rights) that may override specific laws when they conflict.

questions.question: The secular trend in puberty refers to:
options: A historical trend toward earlier onset of puberty across generations
questions.answer: A historical trend toward earlier onset of puberty across generations
questions.explanation: The secular trend documents progressively earlier puberty over the past century, attributed to improved nutrition and reduced childhood illness.

questions.question: Identity achievement (Marcia) is associated with:
options: Crisis followed by commitment after genuine exploration
Adoption of parental identity without exploration
questions.answer: Crisis followed by commitment after genuine exploration
questions.explanation: Identity achievement follows an active exploration period (crisis/moratorium) and results in a personally constructed commitment.

questions.question: Fluid intelligence in late adulthood typically:
options: Shows gradual decline from early adulthood onward
questions.answer: Shows gradual decline from early adulthood onward
questions.explanation: Fluid intelligence — novel problem-solving and processing speed — peaks in the early 20s and declines gradually across adulthood.

questions.question: Selective optimization with compensation (Baltes) describes how older adults:
options: Narrow goals to areas of strength and use strategies to compensate for losses
Substitute physical activity for cognitive activity
questions.answer: Narrow goals to areas of strength and use strategies to compensate for losses
questions.explanation: SOC theory: older adults select fewer goals, optimize performance in chosen areas, and compensate for losses with strategies or aids.

questions.question: Hospice care differs from hospital care for dying patients primarily in its emphasis on:
options: Comfort, dignity, and quality of life rather than cure
questions.answer: Comfort, dignity, and quality of life rather than cure
questions.explanation: Hospice philosophy prioritizes pain management, emotional support, and dignified dying over curative treatments when illness is terminal.

questions.question: Bronfenbrenner's macrosystem refers to:
options: The immediate family and home environment
questions.answer: Cultural values, laws, and ideologies
questions.explanation: The macrosystem is the outermost layer — the cultural context including dominant values, laws, and ideologies that shape all inner systems.

questions.question: The 'good death' concept in contemporary thanatology emphasizes:
options: A sudden death to minimize suffering
Death in a hospital with maximum medical intervention
Dying with dignity, minimal pain, and in one's preferred setting with loved ones
Death at the oldest possible age
questions.answer: Dying with dignity, minimal pain, and in one's preferred setting with loved ones
questions.explanation: A 'good death' involves patient-centered care honoring dignity, pain management, psychosocial support, and alignment with the dying person's values.

questions.question: Piaget has been criticized primarily because his research:
options: Underestimated the cognitive abilities of infants and young children
Focused too much on social development
questions.answer: Underestimated the cognitive abilities of infants and young children
questions.explanation: Subsequent research using more sensitive measures (habituation, preferential looking) found infants and young children capable of more than Piaget's tasks suggested.

questions.question: The strange situation experiment by Ainsworth involved observing infants in response to:
options: Brief separations from and reunions with their caregiver
questions.answer: Brief separations from and reunions with their caregiver
questions.explanation: Ainsworth's Strange Situation procedure assessed attachment by observing infant behavior during brief separations from and reunions with the primary caregiver.

questions.question: The life review process in late adulthood (Butler) involves:
options: Reminiscing and evaluating past experiences to achieve integrity
questions.answer: Reminiscing and evaluating past experiences to achieve integrity
questions.explanation: Butler proposed that older adults naturally engage in life review — reflecting on the past to derive meaning and accept life as it was lived.

questions.question: Longitudinal research on adult intelligence (Seattle Longitudinal Study) showed that most cognitive abilities:
options: Peak in childhood and decline steadily after age 20
Peak in the 30s–40s and show stability or moderate decline through the 60s
questions.answer: Peak in the 30s–40s and show stability or moderate decline through the 60s
questions.explanation: Schaie's Seattle study found most cognitive abilities peak in early middle age and remain relatively stable, with decline accelerating in the 70s–80s.

questions.question: Anticipatory grief refers to grief experienced:
options: Before an expected loss, such as when a loved one has a terminal diagnosis
By individuals who have never experienced loss before
questions.answer: Before an expected loss, such as when a loved one has a terminal diagnosis
questions.explanation: Anticipatory grief occurs during the period leading up to an expected death, involving mourning, planning, and emotional preparation.
